summary: Hack days are valuable for innovation and experimentation, but they create a dangerous illusion about project complexity. The author describes how hack day successes at Rent the Runway led to dramatically underestimated timelines - an '80% complete' rewrite was actually 80% of basic functionality, ignoring half the required features. Similarly, a cool reviews project that took one day to hack became a man-year engineering effort. While acknowledging her own pragmatic approach (shipping tested, production-ready code), she still endorses the value of ambitious hack day moonshots.
key_points: Hack day projects skip tests, edge cases, and scalability concerns, creating a false sense of completion
The '80% done' illusion is particularly dangerous - it often means 80% of basic functionality, ignoring the harder half of requirements
Cool hack day projects can seduce teams into dramatically underestimating production timelines (one day hack → 4 engineers for 3 months)
Engineers can fall so in love with hacks that they neglect regular duties for projects with questionable business value
There's value in pragmatic hack day projects that ship same-day with tests, but moonshot projects still have their place
money_quotes: We can't forget the seductive illusion of the hack.
In reality, the 80% completion was more like '80% of basic functionality completed', which is still very impressive but ignores the 50% of required functionality that goes well beyond basic.
I don't think any of us expected this cool thing that took a day to hack to turn into 4 engineers working for three full months just to get a polished v1.
The hack day siren song can also cause you to fall in love with your hack so much you don't see the cost to making it production-ready.
It's hard to complain when people are working on something they're passionate about, but it's important for a team to feel like they are all working toward the same goal.
My expected 2 hour hack still took me closer to 6 hours end to end.
What's the point in fast-loading images if no one ever views them?
key_insight: Hack day projects create a dangerous illusion of near-completion that can lead teams to dramatically underestimate the true effort required for production-ready software.
